There are many types of consumer reporting agencies, including \r\ncredit bureaus\r\nand specialty agencies (such as agencies that sell information about check writing histories, \r\nmedical records, and\r\nrental                                                            history records). Here is a \r\nsummary of your major rights under FCRA. For more information, including\r\ninformation about additional rights, go to www.consumerfinance.gov/learnmore or write to: Consumer\r\nFinancial Protection Bureau, 1700 G Street NW, Washington, DC 20552.\r\n\r\n⚫  You must be told if information in your file has been used against you. Anyone who uses a credit \r\nreport\r\nor another type of consumer report to deny your application for credit, insurance, or employment – \r\nor to\r\ntake                                                         another adverse action against you – \r\nmust tell you, and must give you the name, address, and phone\r\nnumber of the agency that provided the information.\r\n\r\n⚫  You have the right to know what is in your file. You may request and obtain all the information \r\nabout you\r\nin the files of a consumer reporting agency (your “file disclosure”). You will be required to \r\nprovide proper\r\nidentification, which may include your Social Security number. In many cases, the disclosure will \r\nbe free. You\r\nare entitled to a free file disclosure if:\r\n\r\n🔾 a person has taken adverse action against you because of information in your credit \r\nreport;\r\n\r\n🔾 you are the victim of identity theft and place a fraud alert in your file;\r\n\r\n🔾 your file contains inaccurate information as a result of fraud;\r\n\r\n🔾 you are on public assistance;\r\n\r\n🔾 you are unemployed but expect to apply for employment within 60 days.\r\n\r\nIn addition, all consumers are entitled to one free disclosure every 12 months upon request from \r\neach\r\nnationwide credit bureau and from nationwide specialty consumer reporting agencies. See\r\nwww.consumerfinance.gov/learnmore for additional information.\r\n\r\n⚫  You have the right to ask for a credit score. Credit scores are numerical summaries of \r\nyour\r\ncreditworthiness                                                          based on information from \r\ncredit bureaus. You may request a credit score from consumer\r\nreporting agencies that create scores or distribute scores used in residential real property loans, \r\nbut you will\r\nhave to pay for it. In some mortgage transactions, you will receive credit score information for \r\nfree from the\r\nmortgage lender.\r\n\r\n⚫  You have the right to dispute incomplete or inaccurate information. If you identify information \r\nin your\r\nfile that is incomplete or inaccurate, and report it to the consumer reporting agency, the agency \r\nmust\r\ninvestigate unless your dispute is frivolous. See www.consumerfinance.gov/learnmore for an \r\nexplanation of\r\ndispute procedures.\r\n\r\n⚫  Consumer reporting agencies must correct or delete inaccurate, incomplete, or \r\nunverifiable\r\ninformation. Inaccurate, incomplete, or unverifiable information must be removed or corrected, \r\nusually\r\nwithin    30 days. However, a consumer reporting agency may continue to report information it has \r\nverified as\r\naccurate.\r\n\r\n000000001-DISC                Page 33 of 36 6117540634-EFX-ACR-1026012b0000109c-04272026\r\n\r\n\r\nPrepared for:\r\n\r\nRICHARD L JOHNSON\r\n\r\nDate: April 27, 2026\r\n\r\nConfirmation # 6117540634\r\n\r\n⚫  Consumer reporting agencies may not report outdated negative information. In most cases, \r\na\r\nconsumer                                                      reporting agency may not report \r\nnegative information that is more than seven years old, or\r\nbankruptcies that are more than 10 years old.\r\n\r\n⚫  Access to your file is limited. A consumer reporting agency may provide information about you \r\nonly to\r\npeople with a valid need – usually to consider an application with a creditor, insurer, employer, \r\nlandlord, or\r\nother business. The FCRA specifies those with a valid need for access.\r\n\r\n⚫  You must give your consent for reports to be provided to employers. A consumer reporting agency \r\nmay\r\nnot give out information about you to your employer, or a potential employer, without your written \r\nconsent\r\ngiven to the employer. Written consent generally is not required in the trucking industry. For more\r\ninformation, go to www.consumerfinance.gov/learnmore.\r\n\r\n⚫  You may limit “prescreened” offers of credit and insurance you get based on information in \r\nyour\r\ncredit report. Unsolicited “prescreened” offers for credit and insurance must include a toll-free \r\nphone\r\nnumber you can call if you choose to remove your name and address from the lists these offers are \r\nbased\r\non. You may opt out with the nationwide credit bureaus at 1-888-5-OPTOUT (1-888-567-8688).\r\n\r\n⚫  The following FCRA right applies with respect to nationwide consumer reporting \r\nagencies:\r\n\r\nCONSUMERS HAVE THE RIGHT TO OBTAIN A SECURITY FREEZE\r\n\r\nYou have a right to place a “security freeze” on your credit report, which will prohibit a consumer \r\nreporting\r\nagency from releasing information in your credit report without your express authorization. The \r\nsecurity\r\nfreeze                                                                    is designed to prevent \r\ncredit, loans, and services from being approved in your name without your consent.\r\nHowever, you should be aware that using a security freeze to take control over who gets access to \r\nthe personal and\r\nfinancial information in your credit report may delay, interfere with, or prohibit the timely \r\napproval of any\r\nsubsequent request or application you make regarding a new loan, credit, mortgage, or any other \r\naccount involving\r\nthe extension of credit.\r\n\r\nAs an alternative to a security freeze, you have the right to place an initial or extended fraud \r\nalert on your credit file\r\nat no cost. An initial fraud alert is a 1-year alert that is placed on a consumer’s credit file. \r\nUpon seeing a fraud alert\r\ndisplay on a consumer’s credit file, a business is required to take steps to verify the consumer’s \r\nidentity before\r\nextending new credit. If you are a victim of identity theft, you are entitled to an extended fraud \r\nalert, which is a fraud\r\nalert lasting 7 years.\r\n\r\nA security freeze does not apply to a person or entity, or its affiliates, or collection agencies \r\nacting on behalf of the\r\nperson or entity, with which you have an existing account that requests information in your credit \r\nreport for the\r\npurposes of reviewing or collecting the account. Reviewing the account includes activities related \r\nto account\r\nmaintenance, monitoring, credit line increases, and account upgrades and enhancements.\r\n\r\n⚫  You may seek damages from violators. If a consumer reporting agency, or, in some cases, a user \r\nof\r\nconsumer                                                           reports or a furnisher of \r\ninformation to a consumer reporting agency violates the FCRA, you may\r\nbe able to sue in state or federal court.\r\n\r\n⚫  Identity theft victims and active duty military personnel have additional rights. For more \r\ninformation,\r\nvisit www.consumerfinance.gov/learnmore.\r\n\r\n000000001-DISC                Page 34 of 36 6117540634-EFX-ACR-1026012b0000109c-04272026\r\n\r\n\r\nPrepared for:\r\n\r\nRICHARD L JOHNSON\r\n\r\nDate: April 27, 2026\r\n\r\nConfirmation # 6117540634\r\n\r\nStates may enforce the FCRA, and many states have their own consumer reporting laws.
